questions.question: What did the 2026 systematic review of 39 studies find about attachment change through psychotherapy?

options.text: Attachment never changes — patterns are fixed for life
Most studies reported meaningful shifts toward attachment security, even when attachment wasn't explicitly targeted, with effects most robust in longer-duration treatments
Only medication can change attachment patterns
Attachment only changes if you had a perfect childhood

questions.explanation: The 2026 systematic review (39 empirical studies, 2000-2024) found that psychotherapy produces meaningful shifts toward greater attachment security. Critically, this occurred even in therapies that didn't explicitly target attachment — suggesting the secure therapeutic relationship itself facilitates change. Effects were most robust in longer-duration treatments (6-18+ months). This confirms attachment patterns are responsive to significant relational or therapeutic experiences.

questions.question: What does realistic attachment change look like over time?

options.text: Instant transformation from 'anxious' to 'secure' after one therapy session
Gradual shifts in how intensely patterns activate, building new habits, regression under stress followed by recovery — not linear progress
Complete elimination of all anxiety or need for space
No change possible — you're stuck with your pattern forever

questions.explanation: Attachment change is gradual (6-18+ months minimum), involves building new relational habits that compete with old ones, includes regression under stress (old patterns re-emerge during high stress, then recovery), and is not linear. You're NOT aiming to eliminate anxiety or need for autonomy — you're building capacity to notice and interrupt old patterns. The anxiety still arises, but you respond differently. Expecting instant or perfect change sets unrealistic standards.

questions.question: Why does attachment change require repeated corrective relational experiences rather than just insight?

options.text: Insight is completely useless
Internal working models built over years require accumulated evidence that the old model is inaccurate — one secure interaction doesn't update decades of experience
Repetition has no role in learning
Only willpower matters, not actual relational experiences

questions.explanation: Internal working models ('I am unworthy; others will abandon me' or 'closeness is dangerous') were formed over years through repeated early experiences. Changing them requires accumulated contradictory evidence. For example, 50+ instances of 'I felt abandoned but partner came back' gradually teach 'I can count on partner.' One secure interaction doesn't override decades of data. This is why change is slow — your attachment system evolved to be stable, and sudden shifts would be destabilizing.

questions.question: What should you do if you experience regression to old attachment patterns during a stressful period?

options.text: Conclude you've made no progress and give up
Recognize that regression under stress is normal and expected — old patterns re-emerge when cognitive resources are depleted, then return to new practices as stress reduces
Blame yourself for being a failure
Demand perfect performance from yourself at all times

questions.explanation: Regression under stress is normal and expected in attachment change. When you're stressed, your cognitive resources are depleted (reduced prefrontal control), and your attachment system defaults to old patterns. This doesn't erase progress — it's part of the change process. What matters: you recognize the regression, understand the trigger, practice self-compassion, and return to new behaviors as stress reduces. Over time, regressions become less frequent and shorter.

questions.question: Which statement about attachment change is most accurate based on current evidence?

options.text: Attachment never changes — it's biologically fixed
Reading one book about attachment is sufficient to change your patterns
Attachment patterns can shift toward security through therapy, secure relationships, and deliberate practice, but change is gradual (6-18+ months) and requires patience
Only childhood experiences matter — adult experiences have no impact

questions.explanation: The evidence shows attachment patterns are responsive to significant relational experiences (therapy, secure romantic relationships, corrective experiences) but change is gradual (realistic timeline: 6-18+ months minimum for meaningful shifts). Insight alone (reading about attachment) is insufficient — change requires repeated corrective relational experiences. The 2026 review confirms this: therapy works, but it takes time and consistent engagement. Patterns are not fixed, but they also don't change overnight.
